chapters.heading: A Computer And A Dream
chapters.body: From floppy disks to modding Minecraft and tweaking every phone setting, curiosity hit before rules did. The internet was less a website and more a laboratory.
chapters.quote: Programming felt like magic the second my games actually executed.
chapters.interactionHint: Tap to burst the first wave of memory fragments.

chapters.heading: Tanky, Java, And The Grind
chapters.body: Ten straight days of Java tutorials turned obsession into output. Debugging became routine, and building a game became the first proof that this path was real.
chapters.quote: I wanted to control a player, and I could not walk away until I did.
chapters.interactionHint: Scroll to increase pressure and shard velocity.

chapters.heading: Global Server, Local Constraints
chapters.body: In 2018, HearthCraft started under hard limits: a broken laptop, coding on a phone, and debugging in coin-operated internet booths while time literally ran out.
chapters.quote: I was running a global startup before I was old enough to own a credit card.
chapters.interactionHint: Hold to widen the tunnel and let more light through.

chapters.heading: HearthCraft Became Home
chapters.body: During the pandemic, people were alone. The server grew to over 40,000 players and transformed from game infrastructure into a social anchor.
chapters.quote: It was not just where people played. It was where people felt at home.
chapters.interactionHint: Swipe and watch fragmented parts reassemble into structure.

chapters.heading: Shutdown, Then Signal
chapters.body: Maintaining the server through college built both income and identity, but not forever. After shutting it down in 2025, the lesson remained: build responsibly, teach what you learned, and keep creating.
chapters.quote: Minecraft taught me how to architect systems, communities, and myself.
chapters.interactionHint: Pause here to let old chapters orbit back as echoes.
